old_title: Andy Burnham latest: PM brings back £2 bus cap - but U-turns on tax-free allowance
new_title: PC Harper's widow brands early release 'deplorable' as Burnham to review scheme
old_text:
Andy Burnham has announced he will reinstate the £2 cap on bus fares but has withdrawn suggestions he would increase the tax-free personal allowance as a way to tackle the cost-of-living crisis.
As part of his policies aimed at tackling the cost of living, Mr Burnham has announced plans to cap most bus fares in England at £2 from January.
The nationwide cap on single tickets is £3 until March 2027, but some areas - including London and Greater Manchester, where Mr Burnham set a £2 limit as mayor - have lower top rates for single tickets.
The prime minister had also said that he wanted to bring a "tangible" improvement to households, telling The Times that "the thing I heard the most on the doorsteps" in Makerfield was the tax-free personal allowance threshold.
The personal allowance, which is the amount of income a person can receive annually before starting to pay tax on earnings, has been frozen at £12,570 for five years.
But in a U-turn on Monday, he said any change would be expensive and "difficult given the financial circumstances in which we find ourselves".
Burnham 'open to listening' in way Starmer 'wasn't', says Welsh FM
Welsh first minister Rhun ap Iorwerth has said Andy Burnham is "open to listening, perhaps in a way that Keir Starmer wasn't", especially regarding the devolution of powers.
It comes a day after Mr Burnham told him and the leaders of Scotland and Northern Ireland he was "focused on practical measures, not constitutional changes like further referendums".
Mr ap Iorwerth told BBC Radio 4's Today programme on Wednesday morning: "I had a conversation with Andy Burnham yesterday, congratulated him on becoming prime minister, and we both made it clear to each other that we wanted to have a positive relationship, a constructive relationship.
"I know that he understands devolution for Manchester. I want to help him understand what devolution means in Wales and what tools we need to make it work better.
"So, what powers do we need? I have a mandate to lead a government which stood on a platform of wanting powers over policing and justice to be devolved in Wales, rail powers and funding to be devolved to Wales, the right to be able to control our own natural resources.
"So there is a list, if you like, of things that we need to devolve now in order to move the story of Wales, the story of devolution, on to the next level.
"Now it's clear that we have now a prime minister that is opening, open to listening, perhaps in a way that Keir Starmer wasn't, and I'm eager to do everything that I can to make the case for Wales. I promised to stand up for Wales, and that's exactly what I'm doing."
What Burnham's record on rough sleeping reveals about his first pledge as PM
Darren Jones intervention on energy bill was 'not the most timely or wise', transport secretary hits back
The transport secretary has hit back at former cabinet minister Darren Jones after he posted to social media claiming that the government's new plan to cut VAT from energy bills is "unfunded".
Heidi Alexander told Sky News: "I'm not sure it was either the most timely or wise tweet that Darren has ever put out."
She added: "Darren's a really good friend of mine, and I've got a lot of time for him. He was a great servant to the cabinet and to Keir's government. But you know, it wasn't the most wise or most timely of tweets.
"Government was going through a process of finding the money to be able to pay for the rollout of Digital ID. Andy Burnham has become the prime minister. He said that isn't going ahead, and so that money that was being found across government to fund that program is now instead being used to take VAT off electricity bills."
Government won't say how bus fare cap is funded, says Stride
Conservative shadow chancellor Sir Mel Stride said the government is "not actually specifying where the money is going to come from" as he questioned the financing of prime minister Andy Burnham's bus fares cap.
Asked if he supported Mr Burnham's £2 cap for single bus fares from January, Sir Mel told BBC Breakfast: "I think anything that's about trying to get on top of cost of living pressures, of course, is very important because under this government we've seen very high levels of inflation, amongst other things."
Transport secretary Heidi Alexander said on Wednesday she was "100 per cent" the bus fare cap was fully funded.
She told BBC Breakfast: "There is £454 million pounds of reprioritising money, it's switch spending."
Sir Mel Stride said there was a "broader point here that's really important, which is that under Labour we've ended up with a huge debt, massive servicing costs on that debt, and that means that we've really got, as a government, the government's got to be explaining exactly where all these spending commitments are going to be funded from, and we saw yesterday a commitment on fuel bills, but when it came to the detail, there was no money allocated for it.
"And from what I can tell from this particular move on bus fares, we're in a similar kind of situation whereby they're saying they're going to make some kind of savings within the energy department's budget, but they're not actually specifying where the money is going to come from."
'We will be boldly Labour but we will also be prudently Labour', minister says
The transport secretary has said Andy Burnham has promised to both be "boldly Labour" and "prudently Labour" as he attempts to tackle the cost of living crisis.
Amid questions over which policies the new PM will pursue, and how he will fund them, Heidi Alexander told BBC Radio 4's Today programme: "When we were at Cabinet yesterday, the prime minister was really clear with us that we are going to be boldly Labor, but we're also going to be prudently Labour, and we will need to reprioritise spending."
She added: "Politics is essentially about priorities, as you know, and Andy has been clear that there will need to be some reprioritisation."
£2 bus cap 'shows the direction of travel' under Burnham, says transport secretary
The £2 bus cap is "a signal of intent and shows you the direction of travel of the government under Andy Burnham", the transport secretary has said, insisting it will give people "a little bit of breathing space and help with the cost of living".
Heidi Alexander told BBC Radio 4's Today programme: "We've seen the experience in Greater Manchester where our new prime minister did so much to improve the public transport there. Since he created the B network, brought busses busses back under public control, set a fare cap, had control over timetables, we've seen bus usage go up by 11 per cent.
"But really, what this announcement today is about is giving people a little bit of breathing space and help with the cost of living. We know that there will be a lot of people that get to the end of every month before the next paycheck comes in, and they'll be worrying about whether there's any money left in their bank account.
"And there are millions of people who rely upon busses in England. There's a significant proportion of people that don't have access to a car, and so reducing that bus fare cap down for single journeys from £3 to £2 - on top of the announcement that we made about getting rid of VAT on energy bills from this autumn - I think this is really a signal of intent and shows you the direction of travel of the government under Andy Burnham."
Streeting and Mahmood apologise for joke about early prisoner release caught on hot mic
Defence secretary Wes Streeting has apologised after comments he made about the early release of prisoners were inadvertently picked up by a microphone.
Mr Streeting made the remarks during a conversation with home secretary Shabana Mahmood and the new justice secretary Alex Norris on Tuesday.
In the overheard comments, Mr Streeting suggested to Ms Mahmood that Mr Norris was "the enemy now" and "wants to let them all out". The exchange occurred on Tuesday, a day after Mr Streeting returned to prime minister Andy Burnham's new-look Cabinet.
Taking to X, Mr Streeting clarified that his comment was a reference to a joke shared between former Conservative prime minister Theresa May and ex-chancellor and home secretary Kenneth Clarke.
He wrote: "I hope everyone can see there was no ill intent behind this ribbing of the new justice secretary - it was a reference to an old May/Clarke joke.
"But I appreciate these are very serious issues and am sorry for any offence inadvertently caused by this moment of levity."
Burnham's plans for devolution 'remains to be seen'
Sir Mel Stride said it "remains to be seen" what Andy Burnham means by devolution.
The Tory shadow chancellor said the detail in the prime minister's plans was missing, and "it feels a little bit about musical chairs with politicians."
Asked if he would support the devolution of powers if he had to vote on it, Sir Mel told BBC Breakfast: "So, look, we are a party that brought in devolution. In fact, the only reason that Andy Burnham was ever mayor of Greater Manchester was because of the fact that we set up that mayoralty when we were in power.
"But it remains to be seen exactly what Andy Burnham means by devolution. It can be anything from devolving a little bit more spending, all the way through to sharing taxes that are raised locally, all the way through to actually being able to redesign those taxes, which is a more fundamental reform.
"So we need to really see the detail. But a lot of this, the detail is missing, and it feels to me a little bit about musical chairs with politicians when he speaks about these things, rather than the meaningful and difficult decisions that he's got to take, particularly around spending, particularly around controlling that welfare budget, in order to be able to get that pressure off people at the moment and get our economy moving."
'Not wise' for Darren Jones to criticise government policy
Heidi Alexander has warned Darren Jones it is "not wise" to criticise a policy announcement after he was sacked from cabinet this week.
The former minister had hit out at Andy Burnham's announcement that it was cutting VAT on household electricity bills, a plan which he said was "unfunded".
Speaking to Sky News, the transport secretary said she is "not sure it was either the most timely or wise tweet that Darren has ever put out".
Transport secretary '100% sure' bus fare cap funded
Transport secretary Heidi Alexander said she was "100 per cent sure" the £2 bus fare cap was fully funded.
Some £400 million of the money comes from switching international climate grants to repayable loans.
She told BBC Breakfast: "There is £454 million pounds of reprioritising money, it's switch spending.
"We're not going to be giving those grants to international climate projects in other countries.
"We're going to be making those loans instead, so that money comes back."
She also insisted the cut to VAT on domestic electricity bills was properly financed despite former cabinet minister Darren Jones suggested the money was being switched from an unfunded digital ID scheme.
She said it was "not true to say the money isn't there to implement the changes on electricity bills".
"The Office for Budget Responsibility said that implementing the digital ID programme was going to cost £1.8 billion, about £800 million of that money is actually going to be used to fund that change to cutting VAT off energy bills," she said.
new_text:
The widow of police officer Andrew Harper has said it is "deplorable" that two of his killers are being considered for early release, as new prime minister Andy Burnham promised to review Labour's policy after a backlash.
Lissie Harper - whose 28-year-old husband died after getting caught in a strap attached to the back of a car as three teenage suspects fled the scene of a burglary in 2019 - accused politicians of "making decisions from behind a desk that will have real and lasting consequences" for the families of victims.
News that two of his killers could be released halfway through their sentences sparked fury after PC Harper's mother said such a move was an "insult".
In response, the prime minister said he wanted to make sure "everything has been looked at" before the policy, introduced under Sir Keir Starmer, goes ahead as planned in September.
He told reporters in Bath: "All I can say is I will review the whole of this policy. Nobody can deny the pressure on prison places.
"But I want to assure myself that everything has been looked at before this policy proceeds, and I can give that assurance that every single part of this I will look into with the justice secretary."
Henry Long, then aged 19, was sentenced to 16 years while Jessie Cole and Albert Bowers, both 18 at the time, were handed 13 years in custody in 2020 for PC Harper's manslaughter.
Earlier on Wednesday, transport secretary Heidi Alexander confirmed that Long, the driver of the car, would not be eligible for automatic release, but his two passengers could be.
Responding, PC Harper's wife said: "It has taken me years to rebuild a life that was torn apart in a matter of moments. Years to pick up the pieces left behind by three men whose actions robbed Andrew of his life and stole our future.
"Now - once again - politicians are making decisions from behind a desk that will have real and lasting consequences for victims' families like mine."
She added: "It is deplorable to me that we have reached a point where releasing prisoners early is even considered acceptable. We speak of justice, accountability and standing with victims, yet decisions like this do the opposite."
The chief constable of Thames Valley Police, Jason Hogg said it "feels wrong" for the killers to leave prison early. If they were being sentenced today, they would be handed life sentences under Harper's Law, which was introduced in 2022 following a campaign from the officer's family.
Under the new act introduced by Labour, an estimated 6,000 criminals will be eligible for early release from September to serve the rest of their sentence on licence in the community, including a major expansion of electronic tagging.
Referring to concerns from the family of PC Harper about their early release, the new prime minister said: "I feel for the family, it's an appalling crime that was committed. The main perpetrator will not be subject to any early release. Of course, others involved have potentially been identified.
"But I've spoken to the new justice secretary. He will review the policy together and make further statements in due course."
Conservative shadow justice minister Nick Timothy said it would be "shameful" if PC Harper's killers were released early, adding: "Victims should not have to live in fear all summer waiting for a review."
Prison overcrowding has forced successive governments into making emergency releases, with the Conservative administration's End of Custody Supervised Licence scheme, launched in 2023, releasing over 10,000 inmates between 18 and 70 days early.
This was followed by Labour's SDS40 scheme, which has seen 56,323 offenders released after serving 40 per cent of their prison term between September 2024 and December 2025.
Reforms in the Sentencing Act came after prisons came close to running out of space entirely last year, risking the "total breakdown of law and order" which would leave police unable to make arrests and courts unable to send offenders to custody, the then justice secretary Shabana Mahmood said.
It is based on a package of measures set out in David Gauke's Independent Sentencing Review, which urged the government to take "decisive action" to ensure they are never forced to rely on the emergency releases again.
Unlike SDS40, the Sentencing Act reforms do not exclude those convicted of sex offences, domestic abuse and serious violent offences. However, those serving life sentences, extended sentences and imprisonment for public protection (IPP) jail terms will not be eligible.
Those on standard determinate sentences could be released after a third of their sentence if they behave in prison, but extra days can be added if they break prison rules. They would serve another third under supervision in the community and the last third on licence, facing recall if they reoffend.
Serious offenders jailed for four years or more could be released into supervision at the halfway point.
The measures were broadly welcomed by prison reform experts, who say average sentence lengths have roughly doubled over the past 50 years. However, there have been calls to ensure overstretched probation services are resourced to deal with the surge in offenders in the community.
Pia Sinha, chief executive of the Prison Reform Trust, said, while the concern felt by victims and their families is understandable, the prison population crisis "has not gone away".
Andrea Coomber, chief executive of the Howard League for Penal Reform, warned there is "not a lot of room to manoeuvre" without risking the total breakdown of law and order.
"Successive governments have allowed sentence inflation to run rampant - people are getting longer and longer sentences and the prison places to accommodate them simply do not exist," she added. "The best answer to this challenge would be for Andy Burnham's government to reverse sentence inflation and create a safer and more sustainable prison system."
However, victim's commissioner Claire Waxman said the prime minister's review of the scheme provided an "important opportunity" to ensure safeguards were in place.
Earlier on Wednesday, defence secretary Wes Streeting was forced to apologise after he was caught on camera joking about the scheme.
In a video, published by the news website Guido Fawkes, new justice secretary Alex Norris can be seen walking towards Mr Streeting and home secretary Shabana Mahmood.
Mr Streeting can then be heard calling Mr Norris "the enemy now", adding: "He wants to let them all out."
He later apologised, saying: "I hope everyone can see there was no ill intent behind this ribbing of the new justice secretary, it was a reference to an old May/Clarke joke."
Asked if it was acceptable for members of his cabinet to joke about early release, Mr Burnham said: "No, it's not, and certainly that's been acknowledged, and apologies have been made."
